county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Cape Cod records are exceptionally well preserved due to the region's stable climate and strong local historical societies.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Plymouth Colony territory in 1685. Encompasses all of Cape Cod (15 towns). One of the few MA counties retaining some governmental functions after 2000 abolition. Barnstable County has a regional government with a county commissioners structure. The Cape Cod Commission exercises land-use authority across the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Barnstable County research centers on Cape Cod's maritime and whaling heritage. The Sturgis Library in Barnstable holds the oldest library building in the US (1644) and has extensive genealogical collections including the Lothrop genealogy and Cape Cod family files. The Cape Cod Genealogical Society maintains surname files and publishes 'The Cape Cod Genealogical Society Bulletin.' The Tales of Cape Cod archives in Barnstable preserve oral histories from old Cape families. Town clerks remain the primary record keepers — Sandwich (1637), Barnstable (1639), and Yarmouth (1639) have records from the earliest settlement. The Nickerson Room at Cape Cod Community College holds microfilmed vital records for all 15 Cape towns. Portuguese immigration records are found in Provincetown's Heritage Museum.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Barnstable County Registry of Deeds; online index and images from 1685

source_profiles.source_name: Barnstable County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Barnstable County Registry of Deeds, Barnstable
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed records for all 15 Cape Cod towns from 1685 to present. Includes deeds, mortgages, attachments, and plans.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival — continuous from 1685 with no gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: Barnstable County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Barnstable Probate and Family Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records including wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships for all Cape Cod towns. FamilySearch has digitized records through approximately 1916.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. Early records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Cape Cod Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / NEHGS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records series covering births, marriages, and deaths for Barnstable, Sandwich, Yarmouth, Eastham, and other Cape towns from earliest settlement to 1850.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes preserve data even where originals deteriorated. Town clerk originals also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: Cape Cod Congregational Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Congregational Library & Archives, Boston / NEHGS
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational (Puritan) church records from Cape Cod's founding churches including Sandwich, Barnstable, Yarmouth, and Eastham. Baptisms, marriages, deaths, admissions, and disciplinary records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Strong survival. Many deposited at the Congregational Library in Boston.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Barnstable County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Cape Cod towns. Maritime occupations prominently listed. Massachusetts also had a state census in 1855 and 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All federal censuses survive for Barnstable County; 1890 census lost nationally.

source_profiles.source_name: Barnstable Patriot and Cape Cod Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Sturgis Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Barnstable Patriot (1830+), Yarmouth Register, and other Cape Cod newspapers with obituaries, shipping notices, and whaling voyage reports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival on microfilm; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

source_profiles.source_name: Massachusetts Soldiers and Sailors — Barnstable County
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / Ancestry.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Massachusetts Soldiers and Sailors of the Revolutionary War (17 vols) and Civil War records. Cape Cod men served extensively in the Navy due to maritime skills.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete published compilations; original muster rolls at Massachusetts Archives.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Some early court records were damaged by moisture but remain largely legible.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Hampshire County in 1761 to serve the growing population in western Massachusetts. County government abolished in 2000 but the Registry of Deeds continues to operate. The county includes 32 towns and 2 cities. Berkshire was a frontier area settled later than eastern MA, with many settlers arriving from Connecticut.
county_metadata.research_tips: Berkshire County's western MA location means many families migrated from Connecticut via the Housatonic River valley — always check CT records for earlier generations. The Berkshire Athenaeum in Pittsfield is the primary local research library with extensive genealogical holdings including the Local History and Literature Collection. The Hancock Shaker Village archives (1783-1960) contain membership records, journals, and correspondence for one of the most documented Shaker communities in America. The Berkshire County Historical Society holds family papers and town histories. For the county's industrial era, check Crane & Co. paper mill records (Dalton) and General Electric records (Pittsfield). The Williams College Archives hold records relevant to northern Berkshire families. Researchers should note that many Berkshire towns were originally part of New York grants, creating competing land claims that appear in both MA and NY court records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Berkshire County has three registry districts: Northern (Adams), Middle (Pittsfield), and Southern (Great Barrington); online records available

source_profiles.source_name: Berkshire County Registry of Deeds (Three Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Northern (Adams), Middle (Pittsfield), Southern (Great Barrington)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Berkshire is unique in MA with three separate registry districts. Northern District covers Adams, Cheshire, Clarksburg, Florida, Hancock, Lanesborough, New Ashford, North Adams, Savoy, Williamstown, Windsor. Middle covers Becket, Dalton, Hinsdale, Lee, Lenox, Otis, Peru, Pittsfield, Richmond, Stockbridge, Tyringham, Washington. Southern covers Alford, Egremont, Great Barrington, Monterey, Mount Washington, New Marlborough, Sandisfield, Sheffield.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1761. All three districts have excellent survival.

source_profiles.source_name: Berkshire County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Berkshire Probate and Family Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records including wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Some early volumes show water damage but are legible.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Berkshire County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Berkshire towns including Sheffield (earliest, settled 1726), Great Barrington, Pittsfield, Stockbridge, Lenox, and others. Births, marriages, deaths.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive for towns with surviving records. Some smaller towns have fragmentary early records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hancock Shaker Village Records and Berkshire Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Hancock Shaker Village Archives / Berkshire Athenaeum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Shaker community records including membership rolls, journals, correspondence, and death records for Hancock (1783-1960). Also Congregational, Episcopal, and Methodist church records from Berkshire towns at the Berkshire Athenaeum.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Shaker records are exceptionally well preserved. Church records vary by congregation.

source_profiles.source_name: Berkshire County Court of Common Pleas Records
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / Berkshire Athenaeum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court records including civil suits, criminal cases, naturalization records, and land disputes. Early records often involve boundary disputes with New York.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival for most periods.

source_profiles.source_name: Berkshire County Eagle and Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Berkshire Athenaeum / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Berkshire County Eagle (Pittsfield, from 1789), Berkshire County Whig, and other county newspapers with obituaries, court notices, and local news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensive microfilm holdings at Berkshire Athenaeum; partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Bristol County records are well maintained across the dual registry system (Fall River and New Bedford districts in addition to Taunton).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Plymouth Colony territory in 1685. One of the few MA counties retaining some governmental functions. Has three registry of deeds districts: Northern (Taunton), Southern (New Bedford), and Fall River. Major industrial centers in Fall River (textiles) and New Bedford (whaling, then textiles).
county_metadata.research_tips: Bristol County is essential for Portuguese-American genealogy — New Bedford and Fall River received the largest Portuguese immigrant communities in the US, primarily from the Azores, Madeira, and Cape Verde. The New Bedford Whaling Museum holds crew lists, logbooks, and voyage records documenting thousands of men (including Cape Verdean and Portuguese sailors). The Fall River Historical Society has extensive textile mill employment records and French-Canadian parish registers from the massive Quebecois migration of 1870-1920. Taunton's Old Colony Historical Society holds the county's earliest records from Plymouth Colony days. The Spinner Publications archive documents the region's immigrant communities. For Quaker research, check Dartmouth Monthly Meeting records (1699+) at the New Bedford Free Public Library. The Bristol County Registry of Probate in Taunton has continuous records from 1686.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Bristol County has three registry districts (Northern/Taunton, Southern/New Bedford, Fall River); online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Bristol County Registry of Deeds (Three Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Northern (Taunton), Southern (New Bedford), Fall River District
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Three separate registry districts covering all Bristol County towns. Northern District in Taunton handles Attleboro, Berkley, Dighton, Easton, Mansfield, Norton, Raynham, Rehoboth, Seekonk, Taunton. Southern in New Bedford handles Acushnet, Dartmouth, Fairhaven, Freetown, Mattapoisett, New Bedford, Rochester, Wareham, Westport. Fall River District handles Fall River, Somerset, Swansea.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival across all three districts from 1686.

source_profiles.source_name: Bristol County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Bristol County Probate Court, Taunton / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records including wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships for all Bristol County towns. Digitized on FamilySearch through early 1900s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1686. Early Plymouth Colony-era probate records also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: New Bedford Whaling Museum Crew Lists and Voyage Records
source_profiles.repository: New Bedford Whaling Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Crew lists, logbooks, and voyage accounts documenting whaling crews from New Bedford, the world's premier whaling port. Records identify crew members by name, age, race, and birthplace — essential for tracing Portuguese, Cape Verdean, and African American mariners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptional collection of over 2,000 logbooks and thousands of crew lists.

source_profiles.source_name: Fall River French-Canadian Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Fall River / FamilySearch / PRDH
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) for French-Canadian parishes in Fall River including Notre Dame de Lourdes, Ste. Anne, St. Joseph, and others. Essential for tracing Quebec-origin families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Fall River parishes.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Bristol County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Bristol County towns. New Bedford and Fall River enumerations are extensive with foreign-born population details. State censuses of 1855 and 1865 also available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival for all census years except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Bristol County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records series for Taunton, Rehoboth, Dighton, Swansea, Dartmouth, and other Bristol County towns. Deaths, births, and marriages from town clerk originals.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes preserve records comprehensively. Original town records also survive.

source_profiles.source_name: New Bedford Standard-Times and Fall River Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: New Bedford Free Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: New Bedford Mercury (1807+), Evening Standard, Fall River Daily Globe, and Taunton Daily Gazette. Rich in maritime news, mill notices, immigrant community announcements, and obituaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm holdings at local libraries; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: New York Colony (Martha's Vineyard was under NY jurisdiction 1664-1691)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Island isolation contributed to excellent record preservation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dukes County encompasses Martha's Vineyard and the Elizabeth Islands. Originally under New York jurisdiction as part of the Duke of York's grant (1664-1691), transferred to Massachusetts in 1691. Retains some county government functions. Small population (7 towns). Home to the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah), a federally recognized tribe.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dukes County research requires understanding the island's unique history. Martha's Vineyard was under New York Colony from 1664-1691, so check NY Colonial records for that period — particularly the records of Governor Thomas Dongan. The Martha's Vineyard Museum in Vineyard Haven holds the most comprehensive local collection including whaling records, ship logs, and family papers dating to the 1640s. Edgartown town clerk records begin in 1646. The Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah) maintains tribal genealogical records — contact the tribal office for access. Deaf heritage research is important here: Martha's Vineyard had a hereditary deafness community documented in Nora Ellen Groce's 'Everyone Here Spoke Sign Language.' The Dukes County Registry of Deeds in Edgartown has records from 1695. Island families intermarried extensively, making comprehensive family reconstruction possible but requiring careful attention to cousin marriages and repeated given names.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Dukes County Registry of Deeds at Edgartown; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Dukes County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Dukes County Registry of Deeds, Edgartown
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for all Martha's Vineyard towns and Elizabeth Islands from 1695 to present.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival. Island conditions and small community ensured careful record keeping.

source_profiles.source_name: Dukes County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Dukes County Probate Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records for all Vineyard towns. Wills, inventories, and administrations often reference whaling vessels and maritime property.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1695.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Edgartown, Tisbury, and Vineyard Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Edgartown, Tisbury (Vineyard Haven), Chilmark, and other Vineyard towns. Births, marriages, and deaths from the earliest settlement period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive. Original records survive at town clerk offices.

source_profiles.source_name: Martha's Vineyard Church Records and Wampanoag Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Martha's Vineyard Museum / Congregational Library, Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational church records from Vineyard towns plus records of the Mayhew family's mission to the Wampanoag (1642+). Thomas Mayhew Jr.'s mission produced some of the earliest Native-English bilingual records in America.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Mission records partially survive; Congregational records are strong.

source_profiles.source_name: Massachusetts Soldiers and Sailors — Dukes County
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / Ancestry.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Service records for Vineyard men in the Revolution and Civil War. Island men often served in naval roles due to maritime experience.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete published compilations available.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Dukes County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Martha's Vineyard towns 1790-1950. Small populations make complete enumeration easy to verify. Wampanoag tribal members enumerated separately in some years.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival for all census years except 1890.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Essex County has some of the oldest continuously maintained records in America. The 1914 Salem fire destroyed parts of the city but court records were unharmed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original Massachusetts counties (1643). County government abolished in 1999. Has two registry of deeds districts: Northern (Lawrence) and Southern (Salem). Salem served as the county seat and site of the 1692 witch trials. Major cities include Lynn, Lawrence, Haverhill, Salem, Gloucester, and Newburyport.
county_metadata.research_tips: Essex County is one of the richest counties in America for genealogical research. The Essex Institute (now Peabody Essex Museum) in Salem was founded in 1848 and holds one of the finest collections of early American manuscript materials — family papers, account books, ships' logs, and Essex County court records from the 1630s. The Salem Witch Trials records (1692) are at the Peabody Essex Museum and digitized at the University of Virginia's Salem Witch Trials Documentary Archive. The Phillips Library at PEM has the Essex County court records transcribed in the multi-volume 'Records and Files of the Quarterly Courts of Essex County' (1636-1686). For Lawrence's immigrant communities (Irish, French-Canadian, Italian, Syrian/Lebanese), check the Immigrant City Archives at Lawrence Heritage State Park. Gloucester's Cape Ann Museum holds fishing industry records. Newburyport's Custom House Maritime Museum documents North Shore shipping families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Essex County has two districts: Northern (Lawrence) and Southern (Salem); online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Essex County Registry of Deeds (Two Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Northern (Lawrence) and Southern (Salem) Districts
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Northern District (Lawrence) covers Amesbury, Andover, Boxford, Georgetown, Groveland, Haverhill, Lawrence, Merrimac, Methuen, Newbury, Newburyport, North Andover, Rowley, Salisbury, West Newbury. Southern District (Salem) covers Beverly, Danvers, Essex, Gloucester, Hamilton, Ipswich, Lynn, Lynnfield, Manchester, Marblehead, Middleton, Nahant, Peabody, Rockport, Salem, Saugus, Swampscott, Topsfield, Wenham.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Among the oldest deed records in America. Continuous from 1643 with excellent condition.

source_profiles.source_name: Records and Files of the Quarterly Courts of Essex County
source_profiles.repository: Peabody Essex Museum (Phillips Library) / Published volumes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published 8-volume transcription of Essex County court records (1636-1686) plus manuscript originals. Covers civil suits, criminal cases, land disputes, moral offenses, and guardianship matters. Provides extraordinary detail on everyday colonial life.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes make these records fully accessible. Original manuscripts at PEM.

source_profiles.source_name: Essex County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Essex County Probate Court, Salem / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1638 including wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships. The 'Probate Records of Essex County, Massachusetts' (3 vols, 1916-1920) covers 1635-1681.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival. Among the oldest probate records in America.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Essex County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Salem, Ipswich, Newbury, Gloucester, Andover, Haverhill, and all other Essex County towns. These volumes are foundational for colonial New England genealogy.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published series is comprehensive. Original town records survive in excellent condition.

source_profiles.source_name: Lawrence Immigrant City Archives
source_profiles.repository: Lawrence Heritage State Park / Lawrence History Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lawrence was a planned industrial city (est. 1847) that attracted massive immigration. Archives document Irish, French-Canadian, Italian, Syrian/Lebanese, Lithuanian, and Polish communities. Includes mill employment records, ethnic society records, and the 1912 Bread and Roses Strike documentation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Strong collections held by Lawrence History Center and Lawrence Public Library.

source_profiles.source_name: First Church Salem and Essex County Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Peabody Essex Museum / Congregational Library, Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: First Church Salem (1629) records and other Essex County Congregational, Episcopal, and Quaker meeting records. Salem and Ipswich churches have records from the 1630s. Quaker meetings at Lynn and Salem are documented.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally excellent survival for the oldest churches. Some gaps in smaller congregations.

source_profiles.source_name: Salem Gazette and Essex County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Peabody Essex Museum / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Salem Gazette (1768+), Essex Register, Newburyport Herald, Haverhill Gazette, Lawrence Eagle, and Gloucester Daily Times. Rich maritime and industrial news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensive microfilm at PEM and local libraries; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. County records are well maintained at the county courthouse in Greenfield.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Hampshire County in 1811 to serve the Connecticut River valley towns in northwestern Massachusetts. County government abolished in 1997. Includes 26 towns, many rural and sparsely populated. Deerfield was the site of major colonial-era conflicts with Native Americans.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County research often connects to the larger Connecticut River valley migration pattern — families moved upriver from Hartford and Springfield into Deerfield, Greenfield, and surrounding towns. The Pocumtuck Valley Memorial Association (PVMA) in Deerfield is the premier research repository, holding the Indian House Memorial collection, Deerfield town records, and manuscripts related to the 1704 Deerfield Raid (where over 100 townspeople were captured and marched to Canada). Check Canadian records for descendants of Deerfield captives who remained in Quebec. Historic Deerfield has architectural and family records for one of the best-preserved colonial villages in America. The Greenfield Public Library holds Franklin County newspapers on microfilm. For Turners Falls and Millers Falls industrial records, check the Montague Historical Society. Many Franklin County families descend from original Hampshire County settlers — always search Hampshire probate records for pre-1811 ancestors.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Franklin County Registry of Deeds at Greenfield; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Franklin County Registry of Deeds, Greenfield
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for all 26 Franklin County towns from 1811 to present. For records before 1811, consult Hampshire County Registry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1811. Pre-1811 records in Hampshire County.

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Franklin County Probate Court, Greenfield / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1811 including wills, inventories, administrations. For pre-1811, see Hampshire County probate.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1811. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Franklin County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Deerfield, Greenfield, Shelburne, Northfield, and other Franklin County towns. Deerfield records begin with its first settlement period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive. Some early Deerfield records lost in 1704 raid but most were recompiled.

source_profiles.source_name: Deerfield Raid and Colonial Warfare Records
source_profiles.repository: Pocumtuck Valley Memorial Association, Deerfield
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of King Philip's War (1675-76), the 1704 Deerfield Raid, and French and Indian War activities in the Connecticut River valley. Captive narratives, military muster rolls, and correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: PVMA holds the premier collection. Some published in George Sheldon's 'History of Deerfield.'

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Franklin County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Franklin County from 1820 (first census after county creation). Earlier records under Hampshire County. State censuses of 1855 and 1865 also available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1820 except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Greenfield Gazette and Franklin County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Greenfield Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Greenfield Gazette (1792+), Franklin County Recorder, and Turners Falls Reporter. Local news, obituaries, and agricultural notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm holdings at Greenfield Public Library; partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Springfield's records have been well maintained despite the city's industrial growth and urban renewal.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Hampshire County in 1812 with Springfield as county seat. County government abolished in 1998. Includes 23 towns. Springfield is the largest city in western Massachusetts and was a major manufacturing center (Springfield Armory, 1777-1968).
county_metadata.research_tips: Hampden County's central institution for genealogical research is the Connecticut Valley Historical Museum (now Lyman and Merrie Wood Museum of Springfield History) which holds Springfield city records, directories, and family files. The Springfield Armory National Historic Site archives contain employment records spanning 1777-1968 — one of the longest continuously operated manufacturing facilities in American history. For Irish immigrant research, check the records of Springfield's Catholic parishes, particularly St. Michael's Cathedral (1866+). The Holyoke Public Library has strong materials on the city's French-Canadian and Irish mill communities. The city of Springfield's municipal archives contain licensing, tax, and assessment records. For Westover Air Force Base genealogical inquiries (Chicopee), contact NARA St. Louis. The Springfield Republican newspaper (1824+) is extensively microfilmed. Always check Hampshire County probate records for pre-1812 Hampden County families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Hampden County Registry of Deeds at Springfield; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Hampden County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Hampden County Registry of Deeds, Springfield
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for all 23 Hampden County towns. For pre-1812 records, consult Hampshire County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1812 with no gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: Hampden County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Hampden County Probate Court, Springfield / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1812. Pre-1812 probate filed in Hampshire County, Northampton.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1812. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Springfield Armory Employment and Military Records
source_profiles.repository: Springfield Armory National Historic Site / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records for the Springfield Armory (1777-1968), the first national armory. Includes payroll records, employee rosters, and apprenticeship records. Also military service records for Hampden County residents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensive records preserved by the National Park Service. Some early records at NARA.

source_profiles.source_name: Springfield and Holyoke Catholic Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Springfield / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Springfield, Holyoke, Chicopee, and other Hampden County communities. Irish parishes (1840s+), French-Canadian parishes (1870s+), Italian parishes (1890s+), and Polish parishes (1900s+).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival at the diocesan level. Some parish records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Hampden County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Springfield (1636+), Longmeadow, Westfield, West Springfield, and other towns. Springfield was founded in 1636 as one of the earliest Connecticut River valley settlements.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive for most towns.

source_profiles.source_name: Springfield Republican and Hampden County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Springfield City Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Springfield Republican (1824+) was one of the most influential newspapers in 19th-century America. Also Holyoke Transcript, Westfield News, and other local papers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Springfield Republican extensively microfilmed; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Hampshire County court and deed records in Northampton are in excellent condition.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original Massachusetts counties (1662), though it was originally much larger — encompassing all of western MA. Berkshire (1761), Franklin (1811), and Hampden (1812) were all carved from Hampshire. County government abolished in 1998. Current Hampshire County has 20 towns with Northampton as county seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hampshire County is the 'mother county' of western Massachusetts — Berkshire, Franklin, and Hampden were all formed from it, making Hampshire probate and court records essential for pre-separation research in those areas. The Forbes Library in Northampton has outstanding genealogical collections including the Judd Manuscript (Sylvester Judd's massive notes on early Hampshire County families), town histories, and family papers. Hampshire County probate records begin in 1660 and are among the oldest in western MA. Northampton was home to Jonathan Edwards (First Great Awakening) and his church records from the 1730s-1750s survive. The Five College consortium (Amherst, Hampshire, Mt. Holyoke, Smith, UMass) collectively holds significant manuscript collections — check the Five College Archives portal. Smith College's Sophia Smith Collection has women's and family history materials. The Amherst College Archives hold Dickinson family papers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Hampshire County Registry of Deeds at Northampton; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Hampshire County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Hampshire County Registry of Deeds, Northampton
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for all 20 Hampshire County towns from 1662. Also contains pre-separation records for towns now in Berkshire, Franklin, and Hampden counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. Continuous from 1662 with very few gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: Hampshire County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Hampshire County Probate Court, Northampton / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1660 — the parent probate court for all of western Massachusetts. Critical for pre-1761 Berkshire, pre-1811 Franklin, and pre-1812 Hampden County families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Hampshire County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Northampton, Hadley, Hatfield, Amherst, Easthampton, and other Hampshire towns. Northampton records from 1654 are among the oldest in western Massachusetts.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes comprehensive; original town records also well preserved.

source_profiles.source_name: Jonathan Edwards and Hampshire County Congregational Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Forbes Library, Northampton / Congregational Library, Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational church records from Northampton (First Church, est. 1661), Hadley, Hatfield, and other Hampshire towns. Jonathan Edwards pastored First Church 1729-1750; those records survive. Includes membership rolls, baptisms, marriages, and disciplinary cases.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Strong survival. Many deposited at the Congregational Library in Boston.

source_profiles.source_name: Hampshire County Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / Forbes Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court of Common Pleas and General Sessions records from 1662. Early records cover the entire western MA territory. Includes Shays' Rebellion-era court proceedings (1786-87).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Shays' Rebellion records are well documented.

source_profiles.source_name: Hampshire Gazette and County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Forbes Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Hampshire Gazette (1786+, still publishing), Northampton Courier, and Amherst Record. Local news, court notices, and agricultural reports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Hampshire Gazette has continuous run from 1786; extensively microfilmed.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. As the most populous county in New England, Middlesex has maintained extensive records across multiple court and registry locations.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original Massachusetts counties (1643). County government abolished in 1997. Middlesex is the most populous county in New England and the 22nd most populous in the US. Has two registry of deeds districts: Northern (Lowell) and Southern (Cambridge). Originally the county seat was at Cambridge (near Harvard); Lowell became co-seat. Major cities include Lowell, Cambridge, Somerville, Newton, Framingham, Waltham, and Malden.
county_metadata.research_tips: Middlesex County is critical for early Massachusetts research because Cambridge was the intellectual center of the colony (Harvard, 1636) and many prominent families settled here. The Middlesex County courthouse records in Cambridge and Lowell are voluminous. For Lowell mill worker research, the Lowell National Historical Park archives hold corporation records for the Boott, Merrimack, Lawrence, and other textile mills (1820s-1950s) — including payroll books that name thousands of Irish, French-Canadian, and Greek workers. The Center for Lowell History at UMass Lowell holds the most comprehensive Lowell immigrant research collection. Cambridge Historical Commission has architectural surveys that document homeownership patterns. The Concord Free Public Library holds Thoreau, Emerson, and Alcott family papers plus extensive town records. Lexington and Concord battle records (April 19, 1775) are at multiple repositories. The Woburn Public Library has one of the best local history rooms for Middlesex towns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Middlesex County has two registry districts: Northern (Lowell) and Southern (Cambridge); online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Middlesex County Registry of Deeds (Two Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Northern (Lowell) and Southern (Cambridge) Districts
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Northern District covers Lowell, Billerica, Chelmsford, Dracut, Dunstable, Groton, Pepperell, Tewksbury, Tyngsborough, Westford, and others. Southern District covers Cambridge, Somerville, Newton, Waltham, Watertown, Concord, Lexington, Framingham, Marlborough, and others.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival from 1643. Among the oldest deed records in America.

source_profiles.source_name: Middlesex County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Middlesex Probate Court, Cambridge / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Extensive probate records from 1648 including wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships. Middlesex was one of the most probate-active counties due to high population.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival; FamilySearch has digitized through approximately 1916.

source_profiles.source_name: Lowell National Historical Park Mill Records
source_profiles.repository: Lowell National Historical Park / Center for Lowell History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Textile mill corporation records from Lowell (America's first planned industrial city, est. 1823). Payroll books, employment records, and boardinghouse registers documenting Yankee mill girls (1820s-1840s), Irish workers (1840s+), French-Canadians (1870s+), Greeks (1890s+), and Portuguese (1900s+).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensive records preserved by the National Park Service and UMass Lowell.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Middlesex County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Cambridge, Watertown, Charlestown, Concord, Lexington, Woburn, and all other Middlesex towns. Cambridge and Watertown records from the 1630s are foundational for MA genealogy.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published series is comprehensive. Original town records survive in excellent condition.

source_profiles.source_name: Lexington and Concord Battle Records and Middlesex Military Records
source_profiles.repository: Lexington Historical Society / Concord Free Public Library / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the April 19, 1775 battles at Lexington and Concord including alarm lists, casualty rolls, and depositions. Also Revolutionary War service records and Civil War enlistment records for Middlesex County men.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Lexington and Concord battle records are well preserved across multiple repositories.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Middlesex County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Middlesex County towns. Extremely large enumerations — Lowell, Cambridge, and Somerville schedules are voluminous. State censuses of 1855 and 1865 also available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Lowell Sun, Cambridge Chronicle, and Middlesex Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Pollard Memorial Library (Lowell) / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lowell Courier (1826+), Cambridge Chronicle, Concord Freeman, and dozens of Middlesex County newspapers. Mill town papers carry extensive worker community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm coverage; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: New York Colony (Nantucket was under NY jurisdiction 1664-1691)
county_metadata.record_losses: The Great Fire of 1846 destroyed much of Nantucket Town but most official records were saved from the courthouse. Some church records and private papers were lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Nantucket is coterminous — the town and county are the same entity. Like Martha's Vineyard, Nantucket was under New York jurisdiction from 1664-1691 before transfer to Massachusetts. The island was the world's premier whaling port from the 1720s to 1840s, when the industry shifted to New Bedford. The Wampanoag had a significant presence on Nantucket into the 18th century.
county_metadata.research_tips: Nantucket research is uniquely rewarding because the island's isolation and small population make comprehensive family reconstruction possible. The Nantucket Historical Association (NHA) is the single most important repository — it holds town records, whale ship logbooks, crew lists, Quaker meeting records, and family papers spanning the entire history of the island. The NHA Research Library on Fair Street has an extensive card catalog of Nantucket families. Quaker records are essential: the Society of Friends dominated Nantucket from the 1700s to 1840s, and their meticulous birth/marriage/death records fill gaps in civil registration. Eliza Barney's genealogical notes (NHA collection) document hundreds of Nantucket families. The 1846 Great Fire destroyed parts of the town but official records survived. For Wampanoag genealogy on Nantucket, check the records of the 'praying Indian' communities documented by Zaccheus Macy and others. Ship logs at the NHA document not just whale catches but births, deaths, and marriages that occurred at sea.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Nantucket County Registry of Deeds; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Nantucket County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Nantucket County Registry of Deeds, Nantucket
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for Nantucket from the earliest English settlement (1659). Includes original proprietors' records and Quaker-era transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1659. Survived the 1846 fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Nantucket County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Nantucket Probate Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records including wills, inventories, and administrations. Whaling captains' estates often include detailed ship and cargo inventories.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Nantucket Quaker Meeting Records
source_profiles.repository: Nantucket Historical Association / New England Yearly Meeting Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Society of Friends (Quaker) meeting records including births, marriages, deaths, certificates of removal, and disciplinary minutes. The Quakers dominated Nantucket social and economic life from the early 1700s through the 1840s. Also Congregational and other church records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Quaker records are exceptionally well preserved with meticulous record keeping.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Nantucket to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives / NHA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Nantucket covering births, marriages, and deaths from earliest settlement. Supplemented by Barney genealogical papers at NHA.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes and NHA manuscript collections together provide very comprehensive coverage.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Nantucket County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for Nantucket. Small population makes these easy to search completely. The 1820 census captures the whaling boom peak. State censuses of 1855 and 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Nantucket Inquirer and Mirror
source_profiles.repository: Nantucket Historical Association / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Inquirer and Mirror (1821+, still publishing) is the island's newspaper of record. Contains shipping news, whaling voyage reports, obituaries, and community notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete run at NHA; partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Norfolk County records are well maintained at the courthouse in Dedham.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Suffolk County in 1793. One of the few MA counties retaining some governmental functions. Includes 28 towns in the greater Boston suburban ring. Named for Norfolk County in England. Quincy (birthplace of Presidents John Adams and John Quincy Adams) is the largest city.
county_metadata.research_tips: Norfolk County was carved from Suffolk in 1793, so all pre-1793 records are filed in Suffolk County — this is the single most important fact for Norfolk researchers. The Dedham Historical Society holds town records, Dedham Pottery company records, and family papers for the county's oldest communities. For Adams family research, the Adams National Historical Park in Quincy and the Massachusetts Historical Society hold the Adams Family Papers. The Quincy Historical Society has extensive records for the granite quarrying industry that shaped the city. Norfolk County town histories are exceptionally well documented — start with Dedham's records, which begin in 1635 and are among the most complete in Massachusetts. The Thomas Crane Public Library in Quincy has strong local history collections. For Brookline (historically a Norfolk County town, now in Boston's orbit), check the Brookline Historical Society. Wellesley College Archives hold materials relevant to Norfolk County families with college connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Norfolk County Registry of Deeds at Dedham; online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Norfolk County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Norfolk County Registry of Deeds, Dedham
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records for all 28 Norfolk County towns from 1793. For pre-1793 records, consult Suffolk County Registry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1793 with no gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: Norfolk County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Norfolk County Probate Court, Dedham / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1793. Pre-1793 probate for Norfolk towns filed in Suffolk County. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1793.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Norfolk County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Dedham (1635+), Quincy, Braintree, Weymouth, Milton, and other Norfolk County towns. Dedham records are among the oldest and most complete in Massachusetts.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive. Original records survive at town clerk offices.

source_profiles.source_name: Norfolk County Congregational and Unitarian Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Congregational Library, Boston / Dedham Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records from Norfolk County's founding churches including First Church Dedham (1638), Braintree, Weymouth, and Quincy. Many early Congregational churches became Unitarian in the early 1800s — check both denominational archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Strong survival for Congregational/Unitarian records. Some Baptist and Methodist records less complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Norfolk County Town Tax and Assessment Records
source_profiles.repository: Individual town clerks / Dedham Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town tax lists and assessment records for Norfolk County communities. Dedham's records from 1635 are exceptionally early. Rate lists identify property holders, their trades, and relative wealth.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Varies by town. Dedham records are remarkably complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Dedham Transcript and Norfolk County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Dedham Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dedham Transcript (1831+), Norfolk County Journal, Quincy Patriot Ledger, and other suburban newspapers with obituaries, property transfers, and social news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilm at local libraries; partial digitization through Chronicling America.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Plymouth Colony records are extraordinarily well preserved and have been published in multiple scholarly editions.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created in 1685 when Plymouth Colony was absorbed into Massachusetts Bay Colony. One of the few MA counties retaining some governmental functions. Includes 27 towns. Plymouth was the site of the Pilgrim landing in 1620. The county has two registry of deeds districts: Plymouth (Plymouth) and Old Colony (Brockton).
county_metadata.research_tips: Plymouth County is the starting point for millions of Mayflower descendant researchers. The Plymouth Colony records (1620-1691) are published in Nathaniel B. Shurtleff and David Pulsifer's 'Records of the Colony of New Plymouth' (12 vols) and in the General Society of Mayflower Descendants' 'Mayflower Families' series. The Pilgrim Hall Museum in Plymouth holds the oldest continuously operating museum in America (1824) with Pilgrim-era artifacts and manuscripts. The Plymouth County Registry of Deeds has records from 1685 but town-level records predate this. The General Society of Mayflower Descendants (Plymouth) maintains lineage records and publishes verified genealogies — essential for Mayflower-connected families. The Old Colony Club records are at Pilgrim Hall. For Brockton (shoe manufacturing city), check the Brockton Historical Society and public library for factory and immigrant records. The Duxbury Rural and Historical Society has records of the Standish, Alden, and Brewster families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Plymouth County has two registry districts: Plymouth and Old Colony (Brockton); online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Plymouth County Registry of Deeds (Two Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Plymouth District (Plymouth) and Old Colony District (Brockton)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Two registry districts covering all 27 Plymouth County towns. Plymouth District handles southern towns; Old Colony District (Brockton) handles northern towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1685 in both districts.

source_profiles.source_name: Plymouth County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Plymouth County Probate Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1685 including wills, inventories, and administrations. Plymouth Colony probate records (1633-1685) are published separately.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. Colony-era and county-era records are both well preserved.

source_profiles.source_name: Records of the Colony of New Plymouth (1620-1691)
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / Pilgrim Hall Museum / Published volumes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published in 12 volumes by Shurtleff and Pulsifer. Covers court orders, deeds, wills, vital records, military records, and laws of Plymouth Colony. The foundational source for Pilgrim-era genealogy.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Original manuscripts at Massachusetts Archives; published volumes widely available. Exceptional survival.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Plymouth County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Plymouth, Duxbury, Scituate, Marshfield, Bridgewater, and other towns. Plymouth's records begin with the colony founding in 1620.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes comprehensive. Original records survive at town clerk offices.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Plymouth County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all Plymouth County towns. Brockton enumerations are extensive due to shoe industry workers. State censuses of 1855 and 1865 also available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete survival except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Pilgrim Church Records and Plymouth County Churches
source_profiles.repository: Pilgrim Hall Museum / Congregational Library, Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the Pilgrim church (First Parish Plymouth, 1620) and other Plymouth County Congregational churches. Includes the earliest church records in New England outside the Cambridge/Salem area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Earliest Pilgrim church records are fragmentary but improve significantly from the 1680s onward.

source_profiles.source_name: Old Colony Memorial and Plymouth County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Plymouth Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Old Colony Memorial (1822+), Brockton Enterprise, and other Plymouth County newspapers with obituaries, court notices, and Pilgrim anniversary celebrations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm coverage; partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: The Great Boston Fire of 1872 destroyed 65 acres of the commercial district but the Suffolk County Courthouse and most government records survived. Some early church records and private papers were lost.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original Massachusetts counties (1643). Suffolk includes Boston, Chelsea, Revere, and Winthrop. Boston has been the capital of Massachusetts since the colony's founding. Suffolk County government was effectively merged with Boston city government. Norfolk County was carved from Suffolk in 1793.
county_metadata.research_tips: Suffolk County (Boston) is the most record-rich county in Massachusetts. The Massachusetts Archives at Columbia Point hold state-level records from the colonial period forward — start here for pre-1776 Massachusetts research of any kind. The Boston City Archives at Hyde Park hold municipal records from 1630. The Suffolk County Registry of Deeds has continuous records from 1640 — among the oldest in the Western Hemisphere. The Massachusetts Historical Society on Boylston Street holds manuscript collections of extraordinary importance (Adams, Winthrop, Parkman, Dana family papers). The New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS) at 99-101 Newbury Street is the premier genealogical library in New England and publishes the New England Historical and Genealogical Register (1847+), the oldest genealogical journal in the US. For Boston Irish research, check the Archives of the Archdiocese of Boston for parish registers from the 1790s onward. The Italian North End is documented at the North End Branch Library. The Boston Athenaeum has newspaper and manuscript collections. Suffolk County probate records from the 1630s are among the oldest in America.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Suffolk County Registry of Deeds at Boston; online access via masslandrecords.com — among the oldest deed records in America

source_profiles.source_name: Suffolk County Registry of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Suffolk County Registry of Deeds, Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Continuous deed records for Boston, Chelsea, Revere, and Winthrop from 1640. Among the oldest deed registries in the Western Hemisphere. Also contains pre-1793 records for Norfolk County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extraordinary survival — continuous from 1640 despite the 1872 Great Fire (courthouse survived).

source_profiles.source_name: Suffolk County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Suffolk County Probate Court, Boston / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1636 — the oldest continuous probate records in Massachusetts. Wills, inventories, administrations, guardianships, and family settlements for Boston and surrounding towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Outstanding survival from the 1630s. FamilySearch has digitized through approximately 1916.

source_profiles.source_name: Port of Boston Passenger Records and Immigration Archives
source_profiles.repository: NARA Waltham / Ancestry.com / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Boston was the primary port of entry for Irish immigrants (especially during the Famine, 1845-1852) and received significant Italian, Jewish, Canadian, and other immigrant groups. Passenger manifests, customs lists, and alien registration records. Ancestry has indexed Boston passenger lists.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival at NARA Waltham and on Ancestry.com. Pre-1820 lists are fragmentary.

source_profiles.source_name: Boston Vital Records (City and Published Series)
source_profiles.repository: Boston City Archives / NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published 'Report of the Record Commissioners of the City of Boston' (39 vols) covers births, marriages, deaths, selectmen's minutes, and town records from 1630 to 1822. Supplemented by 'Vital Records of Boston to 1850.'
source_profiles.survival_notes: Boston's published record commissioner reports are one of the great achievements of American historical publishing. Original records survive at Boston City Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Boston Church Records — Old South, King's Chapel, and Catholic Parishes
source_profiles.repository: Congregational Library / Massachusetts Historical Society / Archdiocese of Boston
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records from Boston's founding churches (First Church 1630, Old South 1669, King's Chapel 1686) through Catholic parishes (Holy Cross Cathedral 1803+, St. Patrick's 1836+). Also Quaker, Jewish, and African American church records (African Meeting House, 1806+).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Congregational/Episcopal records strong. Catholic parish records well preserved at the Archdiocese. Some records lost in 1872 fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Suffolk County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for Boston, Chelsea, Revere, and Winthrop. Boston enumerations are massive — the city was a top-10 US city through this period. State censuses of 1855 and 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Boston Globe, Boston Evening Transcript, and Suffolk Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Boston Public Library / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Boston News-Letter (1704, first continuously published newspaper in America), Boston Gazette, Boston Evening Transcript (famous genealogy column 1875-1941), and Boston Globe. The Transcript's 'Notes and Queries' column is a genealogical goldmine.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensively microfilmed at BPL. Boston Evening Transcript genealogy column indexed by NEHGS.

source_profiles.source_name: Boston City Directories
source_profiles.repository: Boston Public Library / Ancestry.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Boston city directories from 1789 listing residents by name with occupation and address. Essential for tracking immigrant families, identifying neighborhoods, and establishing timelines between census years.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Nearly complete run at BPL; Ancestry has digitized many volumes.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Worcester County's records are in excellent condition, supplemented by the extraordinary holdings of the American Antiquarian Society.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from parts of Middlesex, Suffolk, and Hampshire counties in 1731. County government abolished in 1998. The largest county in Massachusetts by area with 60 towns. Worcester is the second-largest city in New England. Has two registry of deeds districts: Worcester and Northern (Fitchburg).
county_metadata.research_tips: Worcester County's greatest genealogical asset is the American Antiquarian Society (AAS) on Salisbury Street in Worcester — founded in 1812, it holds the largest collection of printed materials in America from the colonial period through 1876, including newspapers, almanacs, broadsides, and city directories from across the country. The Worcester Historical Museum (formerly Worcester Society of Antiquity) has local family papers and city records. The Worcester Public Library's Local History Room holds Worcester County genealogies, town histories, and vital records. For Fitchburg's Finnish immigrant community, the Finnish Heritage Society archives are unique. Worcester was a center of abolitionism — the records of the Massachusetts Spy (Isaiah Thomas, 1770s) and the anti-slavery societies are at AAS. Blackstone River valley industrial records (Grafton, Millbury, Uxbridge) document early American manufacturing. The town of Sturbridge, home of Old Sturbridge Village, has re-created rural New England life from the 1790s-1830s with actual period documents in its research library.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Worcester County has two registry districts: Worcester and Northern (Fitchburg); online access via masslandrecords.com

source_profiles.source_name: Worcester County Registry of Deeds (Two Districts)
source_profiles.repository: Worcester District (Worcester) and Northern District (Fitchburg)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Two registry districts covering all 60 Worcester County towns. Worcester District covers southern and central towns; Northern District (Fitchburg) covers Ashburnham, Ashby, Athol, Fitchburg, Gardner, Hubbardston, Leominster, Lunenburg, Petersham, Phillipston, Royalston, Templeton, Westminster, Winchendon.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1731 in both districts.

source_profiles.source_name: Worcester County Probate Court Records
source_profiles.repository: Worcester County Probate Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from 1731 covering all 60 towns. Wills, inventories, administrations, and guardianships. FamilySearch has digitized early volumes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. Continuous from 1731.

source_profiles.source_name: Vital Records of Worcester County Towns to 1850
source_profiles.repository: NEHGS / Massachusetts Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Published vital records for Worcester, Lancaster, Brookfield, Mendon, Oxford, Sutton, and other towns. Lancaster records from 1647 (town predates county). Covers births, marriages, and deaths.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes are comprehensive. Some early Lancaster records damaged in King Philip's War but largely recompiled.

source_profiles.source_name: Worcester Spy, Worcester Telegram, and County Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: American Antiquarian Society / Library of Congress
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Massachusetts Spy (Isaiah Thomas, 1770+) is one of the most important Revolutionary-era newspapers. Also Worcester Telegram, Fitchburg Sentinel, and other county newspapers. AAS holds the finest collection of early American newspapers in existence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: AAS holdings are extraordinary — many newspapers survive nowhere else. Extensively microfilmed.

source_profiles.source_name: Worcester County Congregational and Catholic Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Congregational Library, Boston / Diocese of Worcester
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Congregational church records from Worcester County's earliest towns plus Catholic parish records from Worcester, Fitchburg, and other industrial communities (1830s+). Also Quaker meeting records from the Grafton/Uxbridge area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Congregational records generally strong. Catholic parish records well maintained by Diocese of Worcester.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Worcester County
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Ancestry.com / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1790-1950 for all 60 Worcester County towns. Worcester city enumerations are extensive. State censuses of 1855 and 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890 (lost nationally).

source_profiles.source_name: Massachusetts Soldiers and Sailors — Worcester County
source_profiles.repository: Massachusetts Archives / AAS
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military service records from King Philip's War (1675-76, Lancaster destroyed) through the Civil War. Published compilations plus original muster rolls. AAS holds related manuscript materials.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete published compilations. Original records at Massachusetts Archives.
